intro: Shared writing standards for all UI text. Apply these rules in every skill: component briefs, design audits, flow generation, and analysis.
checklist_intro: Use this handy checklist to confirm content is in the right state for reviews. Before meeting with Jeff or Jessie, or asking for review, ensure you've fixed any content issues during design and checked them off here.

sections.heading: Words to avoid
sections.description: Guidance on what content works best, including text, imagery, and messaging.

banned_terms.rationale: Don't refer to Actian as 'we' or 'us'.

banned_terms.do: Add a row to the Allow / Deny List
banned_terms.dont: Add a row to the Black/Whitelist

banned_terms.rationale: Use the action the user is actually doing.
Avoid alarming language for normal flows.

banned_terms.do: Verify that the value is provided.
banned_terms.dont: Ensure you provided the correct value.

sections.heading: Capitalization
sections.description: Don't overuse capitalization as it makes copy harder to read and de-emphasizes the important content.

rows.Sentence case: Functional text (Section headers, Checkboxes, radios, badges, sliders, toggles, tooltips, tags, pills, toasts, loading indicators)
Proper nouns and names of people or products (within sentence case body)

examples.do: Add a row to the catalog. List it on the page.
examples.dont: Add a row to the Catalog. List it on the Page.

examples.do: Add a row to the MyCatalog page.
examples.dont: Add a row to the MyCatalog Page.

sections.heading: Writing style
sections.description: Use present, active tense in all possible cases. In some cases, you can use passive voice if the focus needs to be on a system instead of a person.

table.when: All cases except where passive is needed.
table.example: Only an administrator can create a warehouse.

table.when: Use only when focus is not on a person.
table.example: The database needs to be shut down.

subsections.title: Tone
rules: Use simplest terms; don't try to 'beautify' with alternatives.

examples.dont: Click here to submit the form

subsections.title: Personification
subsections.rule: Do not personify the product as the subject of the text. Keep the user in focus, rather than the product.

examples.do: In the Delivery Information window, specify the name of the sender.
examples.dont: The Delivery Information window allows you to specify the name of the sender.

examples.do: Use this menu to create diagrams.
examples.dont: This menu enables you to create diagrams.

sections.heading: Numerical formatting
sections.description: Standard formats for numbers, currency, dates, and times across the product.

rules.format: Use uppercase AM or PM, without periods
Round down to the largest and most recent date or time.

sections.heading: Punctuation
sections.description: To help readers scan text at a glance, avoid using punctuation in places where it isn't necessary.

subsections.title: Periods
subsections.rule: Avoid using periods on solitary sentences within UI elements listed below.
do_use_in: Sentence followed by a link (links themselves should not be full sentences)

subsections.title: Commas
subsections.rule: Use the Oxford comma — place a comma after the second-to-last item in a series, before the conjunction.

examples.do: You can select green, blue, or both.

subsections.title: Exclamation points
subsections.rule: Use exclamation marks only positively, not negatively. No more than one per page or window.

sections.heading: Prepositions
sections.description: Prepositions indicate relationships between different elements, actions, or concepts. Proper use maintains clarity and precision; misuse leads to confusion or overly complex sentences.

subsections.title: Conciseness — minimize unnecessary prepositions
subsections.rule: While prepositions provide important context, excessive use clutters sentences. Always aim for simplicity.

examples.dont: The settings for managing notifications on your profile.

subsections.title: Accuracy — clarify the relationship
subsections.rule: Prepositions should clearly express the relationship between actions, objects, or concepts.

subsections.title: Avoid vague prepositions
subsections.rule: Choose prepositions that accurately describe the relationship. Be specific.

examples.do: Details on how to configure settings

subsections.title: Keep prepositional phrases short
subsections.rule: Long chains of prepositional phrases confuse users and decrease readability. Break them up.

examples.do: Click the link in your email to view your order details.
examples.dont: Click on the link in the email for information about your order.

subsections.title: Consistency — standardize common phrases
subsections.rule: Frequently used phrases should be consistent across content.

examples.do: Save the changes to your profile.
examples.dont: Save the changes on your profile.

subsections.title: Clear action relationships
subsections.rule: When giving instructions, prepositions should make the action clear.

examples.do: Drag the file into the folder
examples.dont: Drag the file to the folder

subsections.title: Limit directional prepositions in instructional text
subsections.rule: Avoid overly directional prepositions unless critical to spatial understanding.

sections.heading: Acronyms
rules: Spell out an acronym the first time it's mentioned. On subsequent references, use the abbreviation.
If the abbreviation isn't clearly related to the full version, specify in parentheses after the spelled-out version in the first instance.
Fully capitalize all letters in abbreviations: AM/PM, HTML, OK (not Ok or Okay).
If an abbreviation might not be known to the target audience, spell it out in full the first time. Don't spell out commonly-known abbreviations.
If well-known to the average client (e.g., CIC, COE), use without spelling out on first reference.

sections.heading: Plurals
rules: Make sure items are correctly used as plural or singular nouns as appropriate.
Do not use '(s)' or '/s' after a noun to make it plural. If you must indicate both forms, use 'one or more.'

sections.heading: Abbreviations and articles
rules: Don't speak like a robot; use 'a,' 'an,' and 'the' articles.
Use the default JRE to run a shell instance.
